summary: Validated decision-gated record. The Session Manager watchdog timeout pair now has baseline existence on Win25H2Clean, exact ntoskrnl string hits plus current-build Ghidra fallback artifacts, repo-side PoFx pseudocode that ties the pair to directed power watchdog timeout globals, a successful reboot-verified boot trace baseline, a host-side ETL registry review that proves repeated boot-time access to Session Manager\Power, a working Procmon boot-log capture that reproduces adjacent Session Manager\Power traffic from System during boot, a DcomLaunch attribution package that narrows the svchost lead to the service host group containing Power, prior S1-specific Procmon follow-ups that failed to leave decisive in-guest artifacts, and a new tools-hardened lightweight ETW S1 follow-up that still lost the guest before a usable exact-value capture could be completed. The pair remains Class B because the current VMware baseline is S1-only and still does not provide a reliable decisive runtime path for the exact watchdog values.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: The tools-hardened lightweight ETW follow-up confirmed that S1 is available on this VM, but the sleep transition still pushed VMware Tools and the guest shell out before a usable exact-value ETW bundle could be completed, so the current environment still cannot close the watchdog lane decisively.
validation_proof.notes: The runtime story now includes the ETL review, the Procmon boot-log review, a targeted post-boot DcomLaunch/Power trigger, two older S1-specific Procmon attempts, and a newer tools-hardened lightweight ETW follow-up. All of them confirm the candidate surface is real, but the current VMware environment still does not produce a decisive exact live read of WatchdogResumeTimeout or WatchdogSleepTimeout.

setting.name: Session Manager Watchdog Timeouts
setting.casual_explanation: These values appear to control watchdog timeout behavior around directed power transitions. They are not normal user-facing power options and are being tracked as a low-level research lane rather than a supported tweak.
setting.why_people_change_it: Advanced researchers may look at these values when investigating resume and sleep watchdog behavior, power transition timing, or kernel power management internals.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: The pair is kernel-adjacent and boot-relevant. Cross-layer evidence is strong, but the current VMware baseline still does not provide a reliable decisive S1 runtime path for the exact watchdog values, so the lane remains decision-gated at Class B.
setting.professional_notes: The tracked values are HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\Power\WatchdogResumeTimeout and WatchdogSleepTimeout. A clean baseline export on Win25H2Clean confirmed 120 and 300 respectively, with adjacent PowerSettingProfile = 0. The current-build ntoskrnl.exe string passes found exact hits for both names, and the improved Ghidra fallback preserved reviewable artifacts even when natural function boundaries were weak. Repo-side PoFxInitPowerManagement pseudocode then linked the pair to PopFxDirectedPowerUpTimeoutMs and PopFxDirectedPowerDownTimeoutMs. A successful host-driven boot trace on baseline-20260327-regprobe-visible-shell-stable preserved the same 120/300 values after reboot and captured the ETL to host staging. A follow-up host-side xperf registry review then showed repeated boot-time access to Session Manager\Power by System (PID 4) and later svchost.exe (PID 980), including adjacent SystemPowerPolicy and ShutdownOccurred activity. A dedicated Procmon boot-log lane on the same baseline then proved that Procmon can convert the boot log into a large PML and CSV for this family, with 18 filtered hits from System (PID 4) on the same adjacent Session Manager\Power values. A targeted post-boot Procmon probe against the DcomLaunch/Power lane stayed shell-safe, produced a real PML and CSV, and still returned zero matching reads for the watchdog pair or the adjacent Session Manager\Power values. The older direct and scheduled-task S1 Procmon attempts both failed to leave decisive in-guest artifacts. The newer tools-hardened lightweight ETW follow-up then retried the lane with lighter registry tracing and split start/trigger/stop phases on RegProbe-Baseline-ToolsHardened-20260330. That follow-up confirmed S1 support again, but the sleep transition still pushed VMware Tools and the guest shell out before a usable exact-value ETW bundle could be completed. This narrows the blocker: the pair remains Class B not because the evidence is weak, but because the current S1-only VMware environment still cannot produce the decisive exact live-read lane.

allowed_values.label: Observed Win25H2Clean baseline
allowed_values.meaning: The clean VM baseline currently exposes WatchdogResumeTimeout = 120 and WatchdogSleepTimeout = 300.

allowed_values.label: Undocumented custom DWORD pair
allowed_values.meaning: The evidence so far only proves that the pair exists and flows through the power-management path. A safe non-default profile is not validated yet.

targets.notes: Keep the pair together. The current evidence is strongest when WatchdogResumeTimeout and WatchdogSleepTimeout are treated as one lane.

app_current_implementation.provider_source: not currently shipped in the app
app_current_implementation.notes: There is no current RegProbe provider or UI mapping for this candidate. The pair is still in research-only intake.

windows_defaults.label: Observed Win25H2Clean baseline
windows_defaults.applies_to: Current Win25H2Clean baseline with the Session Manager Power key populated

states.rationale: Leave the currently observed watchdog timeout pair unchanged until a stronger supported mapping exists.

windows_defaults.notes: PowerSettingProfile = 0 was also present in the same baseline export, but it remains adjacent context rather than part of this pair.

recommended_profiles.label: Observed baseline

states.rationale: Keep the pair at the current observed baseline while evidence gathering continues.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want to document the current baseline without inventing a non-default tuning profile.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want a shipped tweak or a claimed performance profile.
tradeoffs: Preserves the current observed watchdog pair.
Keeps the lane documentation-only until a supported mapping exists.

recommended_profiles.label: Research candidate pair

states.rationale: Treat the pair as an active research target rather than a validated preset.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You are collecting additional runtime or static evidence for the watchdog timeout pair.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You need a documented end-user recommendation.
tradeoffs: Keeps the lane active for further research.
Does not provide a validated non-default value pair yet.

evidence.title: Win25H2Clean Session Manager Power baseline export
evidence.summary: A clean VM baseline export confirmed WatchdogResumeTimeout = 120 and WatchdogSleepTimeout = 300 under Session Manager\Power, with adjacent PowerSettingProfile = 0.

evidence.title: Current-build ntoskrnl Ghidra fallback for the watchdog timeout pair
evidence.summary: The current-build ntoskrnl.exe pass found exact string hits for WatchdogResumeTimeout and WatchdogSleepTimeout and preserved reviewable fallback artifacts for the next-gate watchdog lane.

evidence.title: Repo PoFxInitPowerManagement pseudocode lead
evidence.summary: The repo-side pseudocode ties PopWatchdogResumeTimeout and PopWatchdogSleepTimeout to the directed power-up and directed power-down watchdog timeout globals, giving the pair a subsystem-level semantic lead.

evidence.title: Win25H2Clean reboot-verified watchdog timeout boot trace
evidence.summary: A successful host-driven boot trace on the current visible-shell baseline confirmed a real reboot cycle, preserved the same 120/300 values after boot, and kept shell health intact.

evidence.title: Host-side ETL registry review for Session Manager Power
evidence.summary: A filtered xperf dump of the successful boot trace proved repeated boot-time access to Session Manager\Power by System and later svchost.exe, with adjacent SystemPowerPolicy and ShutdownOccurred activity. The exact watchdog pair names were still absent in the parsed event payloads.

evidence.title: Win25H2Clean Procmon boot-log review for Session Manager Power
evidence.summary: A baseline-only Procmon boot-log lane successfully converted a large boot PML and CSV, then filtered 18 hits from System on adjacent Session Manager\Power values such as SystemPowerPolicy and ShutdownOccurred. The exact watchdog pair still did not appear in the filtered Procmon boot-log hits.

evidence.title: Win25H2Clean sleep capability for the watchdog lane
evidence.summary: The current VMware baseline exposes only Standby (S1). S3, hibernate, and S0 low power idle are unavailable, which constrains the next exact live-read attempt to an S1 transition or a different environment.

evidence.title: DcomLaunch attribution for the watchdog svchost lead
evidence.summary: The ETL svchost lead was narrowed to the DcomLaunch service host group, and the current VM baseline confirmed that the same group hosts the Power service.

evidence.title: Targeted post-boot Procmon trigger for the DcomLaunch Power lane
evidence.summary: A shell-safe post-boot Procmon capture targeted the DcomLaunch/Power lane with tasklist, powercfg, and service queries. It produced a real PML and CSV but still returned zero matching reads for the watchdog pair or adjacent Session Manager Power values.

evidence.title: S1-specific Procmon follow-up for the watchdog lane
evidence.summary: An S1-specific Procmon attempt tried to force the only available sleep state on the current VMware baseline. Shell health recovered, but the guest-ops channel dropped during the transition, no usable in-guest Procmon artifacts survived, wake history stayed at zero, and post-checks did not show a fresh sleep/resume Kernel-Power event.

evidence.title: S1 scheduled-task Procmon follow-up for the watchdog lane
evidence.summary: A second S1-specific attempt moved the payload into a guest-side scheduled task so the host no longer depended on a long-lived guest-ops session. The lane still produced no usable in-guest artifacts, the task was not present when queried after recovery, wake history stayed at zero, and no fresh sleep/resume Kernel-Power output appeared.

evidence.title: Tools-hardened lightweight ETW S1 follow-up for the watchdog lane
evidence.summary: A tools-hardened lightweight ETW follow-up retried the watchdog lane with split trace phases on RegProbe-Baseline-ToolsHardened-20260330. The VM still exposed only S1, and the guest dropped out during the sleep transition before a usable exact-value ETW bundle could be completed.

evidence.title: Kernel power next-gate intake summary
evidence.summary: The 2026-03-28 intake review ranked the watchdog pair above the Executive worker-thread values because the baseline existence, exact string hits, and Ghidra artifacts were already stronger.

decision.why: The watchdog timeout pair now has cross-layer evidence: a clean baseline export, exact current-build ntoskrnl string and Ghidra fallback artifacts, repo-side PoFx pseudocode, a successful reboot-verified boot trace, a host-side ETL registry review, and multiple runtime attempts across Procmon and lightweight ETW. The current blocker is no longer lack of research depth. It is that the current S1-only VMware environment still does not provide a reliable decisive exact-value runtime path for the watchdog pair.
blocking_issues: The ETL review, the Procmon boot-log review, the targeted post-boot DcomLaunch/Power trigger, and the newer tools-hardened lightweight ETW S1 follow-up still stop short of a decisive exact live read of WatchdogResumeTimeout and WatchdogSleepTimeout.

The current Win25H2Clean VMware baseline only exposes Standby (S1), and repeated S1-specific follow-ups still fail by dropping guest ops or guest shell state before the decisive runtime bundle can complete.

open_questions: Can the exact watchdog pair be captured in a different validation environment with a more reliable sleep/resume path than the current S1-only VMware baseline?
Does a better symbol or binary target produce a cleaner current-build code path than the current forced-boundary ntoskrnl fallback?
Should this pair ever become a shipped tweak, or should it stay documentation-only even if semantics converge?
Is a different validation environment needed if the decisive trigger depends on S3, hibernate, or modern standby semantics?

tested_on.notes: Baseline export, successful host-driven boot trace, and the older S1-specific Procmon follow-ups ran on baseline-20260327-regprobe-visible-shell-stable. The final tools-hardened lightweight ETW retry ran on RegProbe-Baseline-ToolsHardened-20260330 and still lost the guest during the S1 transition.
